title_en: What I Learned About Billionaires at Jeff Bezos’s Private Retreat
title_zh: 我在杰夫·贝索斯的私人度假地所学到的关于亿万富翁的事
rubric_en: For the richest men on Earth, everything is free and nothing matters.
rubric_zh: 对地球上最有钱的人来说，一切都是免费的，也没有什么真正重要。

paragraphs.en: At the end of Paul Thomas Anderson’s 2007 movie, There Will Be Blood, Daniel Day-Lewis’s oil-baron character, old now and richer than Croesus, beats Paul Dano’s preacher to death with a bowling pin. Dano’s Eli Sunday, a nemesis of Day-Lewis’s Daniel Plainview during his seminal, wealth-building years, has come to sell Plainview the oil-rich land that he once coveted. But Plainview doesn’t need the land anymore, because—as he explains in one of the most famous monologues in modern cinema—he has sucked out all the oil hidden beneath it from an adjoining property, like a milkshake.
paragraphs.zh: 在保罗·托马斯·安德森执导的2007年电影《有血有泪》结尾，丹尼尔·戴-刘易斯饰演的石油大亨角色——早已暮年的他，财富胜过克洛伊斯（Croesus）——用保龄球瓶将保罗·达诺饰演的传教士打死了。达诺扮演的伊莱·桑德（Eli Sunday），在丹尼尔·普莱恩维尤（Daniel Plainview）辉煌致富的关键岁月里一直是他的死对头，来向普莱恩维尤兜售那片他曾垂涎的富含石油的土地。可普莱恩维尤不再需要这片土地，因为——正如他在现代电影史上最著名的独白之一中所说——他已经从隔壁地块把那里的油给全部“吸”了出来，像摇奶昔一样把里面的油都吸走了。

paragraphs.en: Desperate for money, Eli begs for a loan. Instead, Plainview chases him around a bowling alley and murders him with great enthusiasm. Once it’s over, a butler comes to see what all the noise was about. “I’m finished,” Plainview yells.
paragraphs.zh: 伊莱因缺钱而恳求借款。普莱恩维尤却没有借，而是带着近乎狂热的兴致把他赶进保龄球馆追逐，并杀了他。等这一切结束后，一个管家走进来，想看是什么动静。普莱恩维尤大喊：“我完了。”

paragraphs.en: No matter how many times I watch that movie, and I watch it a lot, I have never once taken those words to mean I’m done for. There will now be consequences for my actions. Quite the opposite: They mean that Plainview has completed his journey, through the acquisition of wealth and power, to a realm outside the moral universe. He’s finished, in other words, pretending that the rules of human society apply to him.
paragraphs.zh: 不管我看过多少次这部电影——而我确实看了很多次——我从未把那句台词当成“我完蛋了”来理解。意思并非我会因此承受后果。恰恰相反：它意味着普莱恩维尤在财富和权力的攀升中完成了一段旅程，进入了道德宇宙之外的领域。换句话说，他已经玩完了，终止了自己假装人类社会规则也适用于自己的那种姿态。

paragraphs.en: In 2018, I was a guest at Jeff Bezos’s Campfire retreat in Santa Barbara, California. It’s an annual event in which the Amazon founder invites 80-plus guests—celebrities, artists, intellectuals, and anyone else he thinks is interesting—to spend three nights at a private resort. I had recently been approached by Amazon about moving my film-and-television business over from Disney, and although I had declined (or maybe because I had declined), Bezos’s team invited me to Campfire, perhaps keen to impress me with the power of his reach.
paragraphs.zh: 2018年，我是杰夫·贝索斯在加州圣芭芭拉举办的“Campfire”（营火）私密静修活动的一位来宾。这是一次年度活动，亚马逊创始人会邀请八十多位客人——名人、艺术家、知识分子，以及任何他认为有趣的人——在一个私人度假村住三晚。我最近刚被亚马逊邀约，将我的影视业务从迪士尼转过去；虽然我拒绝了（或许正是因为我拒绝了），贝索斯的团队还是邀请我参加Campfire，也许是想让我亲眼见识一下他影响力的尺度。

paragraphs.en: From the March 2024 issue: The rise of techno-authoritarianism
paragraphs.zh: 来源于2024年3月刊：技术威权主义的崛起

paragraphs.en: On a warm October Thursday, a fleet of private jets was dispatched to airports in Van Nuys and New York to shepherd guests to Santa Barbara in style. At that point I had only a vague sense of who else was coming—famous people, rich people, influential people, and me. A guest list, I was told, would be given to us once we arrived. Families were invited; an on-site nanny would be provided for each child.
paragraphs.zh: 在一个温暖的十月周四，一支私人飞机机队被派往范努伊斯和纽约的机场，以尽显体面地接送宾客前往圣巴巴拉。那时我对来宾名单里的其他人只是有个模糊印象——名人、富豪、有影响力的人物，还有我一个人。有人告诉我，等我们到了之后会把名单给我们。客人中包括家庭成员；每个孩子都会配备一名现场保姆。

paragraphs.en: So my wife and I got our two children from Austin to Los Angeles and took a 45-minute jet ride north, with a television mogul and a comedian on board. Bezos had bought out the entire Biltmore resort for the weekend, as well as the beach club across the street. He had brought in a security firm from Las Vegas to ensure our safety and privacy. Even the weather felt expensive, and when we were shown to our rooms, the designer gift bags we found were filled with luxury goods.
paragraphs.zh: 于是我和妻子把两个孩子从奥斯汀带到洛杉矶，然后乘坐了45分钟的包机向北飞行，机上还有一位电视大亨和一位喜剧演员。贝索斯把整个比尔特摩尔度假村整周末都包下了，连街对面的海滩俱乐部也一起买断了。他还请来一家来自拉斯维加斯的安保公司，负责我们的安全和隐私。连天气都显得很奢侈，而当我们被带到客房时，发现赠送的设计师礼袋里装满了奢侈品。

paragraphs.en: Alexandra Petri: The 10 things the Bezoses are almost certainly grateful for each morning
paragraphs.zh: 亚历山德拉·彼得里：贝索斯夫妇每天早上几乎必然心存感激的十件事

paragraphs.en: Each morning, we gathered in a lecture hall to hear presentations. If you’ve ever seen a TED Talk, you understand the format. The year I went, a sitting Supreme Court justice was interviewed, and a neurologist talked about technological advances in prosthetics. In the afternoons and evenings, we were encouraged to exchange ideas over drinks and four-course meals, with no set purpose—to network, in other words, with some of the most rarefied talent on Earth. The most common question I heard was “Why am I here?”
paragraphs.zh: 每天早上，我们都会在一个演讲厅里集合听汇报。如果你看过 TED 演讲，就能理解这种形式。那一年，来的人有一位在任的最高法院法官接受了采访，还有一位神经科学家讲述了假肢技术的最新进展。下午和晚上，我们被鼓励在酒会上与四道菜晚餐时随意交流，不设固定议题——也就是想当然地说，用更世俗的话就是与地球上最顶尖的人才建立关系。听到最多的问题是：“我为什么会在这里？”

paragraphs.en: “Why am I here?” asked the 1980s hair-metal singer. “Why am I here?” asked the Pulitzer Prize–winning novelist, the famous anthropologist, the presidential historian. Only the movie stars and the billionaires didn’t ask: They had done this kind of thing before. It turns out there is a circuit of idea festivals. Many tech billionaires host one, and if you find yourself on the right list, you can spend much of the year traveling the world, eating Wagyu, and discussing how to make the world a better place with the most famous talk-show host in history.
paragraphs.zh: “我为什么会在这里？”问的是这位 80 年代发型摇滚歌手。“我为什么会在这里？”问的是这位普利策奖小说家、那位著名人类学家、那位总统历史学家。只有电影明星和亿万富翁没有问：他们早就参加过这类活动。事实是，世界上有一条“创意盛会”线路。许多科技亿万富翁都会主办其中一个，如果你恰好在正确的名单上，就能一整年在世界各地奔波，品尝和牛，和历史上最有名的脱口秀主持人讨论如何让世界变得更美好。

paragraphs.en: That’s how the weekend started. Here’s how it ended: My wife broke her wrist slipping on wet grass, and both children and I came down with hand, foot, and mouth disease. This is not a joke. One of us went home with her arm in a sling; the other three developed itchy, painful red blisters all over our faces and extremities. If you’re looking for a sign from God as to whether hanging out with the richest man on Earth is right for you, pay attention when he sends you not one plague, but two. Suffice it to say we have never been back to Campfire, nor have we ever been invited.
paragraphs.zh: 周末就是这样开始的。结尾则是这样的：我的妻子在湿草地上滑倒，手腕扭伤；而我和两个孩子都得了手足口病。这绝非玩笑。我们中的一个人带着手臂吊带回了家；另外三个人的脸和四肢上都长出了又痒又痛的红色水疱。如果你在寻找“上天的信号”来判断和地球上最富有的人来往是否适合你，就请留心：当他给你的不是一场瘟疫，而是两场瘟疫时，答案已经显现。可以这么说，我们从未再去过 Campfire，也从未再受邀前往。

paragraphs.en: At drinks on the second night, the head of a major talent agency asked me what I thought of the weekend. I said, “I’ve spent my whole career trying to figure out how the world works. I didn’t realize I could just come here and ask the people who ran it.” On some level I was kidding. The lead singer of an alt-country band didn’t run the world, nor did a noted author who would later be accused of impropriety. But finding myself at that resort by exclusive invitation, I now knew exactly what people meant when they talked about the elite.
paragraphs.zh: 第二晚的酒会上，一位大型经纪公司负责人问我对这个周末的看法。我说：“我整个职业生涯都在试图弄清这个世界是怎么运转的。我没想到我只要来这里，直接向那些真的在操盘的人请教就行。” 从某种意义上我是在开玩笑。那个另类乡村乐队主唱并不真正掌控世界，那位后来还会被指控不当行为的著名作家也不是。但当我发现自己凭着独一无二的邀请坐在那个度假胜地时，我终于明白了人们谈论“精英”时到底指的是什么。

paragraphs.en: Sitting in the lecture hall, pencils out, listening to a famous chef explain his humanitarian work, it was easy to feel like the solution to the world’s problems lay within our grasp. And yet, looking around at faces I had only ever seen in a magazine or on-screen, I had an unsettling revelation: This is the hubris of accomplishment. To be declared a genius at one thing is to begin to believe you are a genius at everything.
paragraphs.zh: 坐在讲堂里，手里的铅笔放在一边，听一位著名厨师讲解他的慈善人道主义工作时，很容易让人感觉世界问题的解决方案似乎就握在我们手里。然而，当我环顾四周，看见那些我过去只在杂志或屏幕上见过的面孔时，我有了一个令人不安的顿悟：这就是成就带来的骄傲。一个人在某个领域被称为天才，就会开始相信自己在一切事情上都是天才。

paragraphs.en: Here we were, 80 individuals with a combined net worth that was greater than a small city’s yet infinitesimal compared with the wealth and dominion of our host. How did he view this exercise—as a first step toward changing the world, or as a performative display of his reach and influence?
paragraphs.zh: 我们80人参加这里，合计净资产超过一座小城，却在与我们主人那样规模的财富和权力相比时几乎微不足道。他如何看待这一切——是把这次活动当作改变世界的第一步，还是当作他影响力和权势的表演秀？

paragraphs.en: Bezos was everywhere that weekend—in a tight T-shirt, laughing too loudly, arms thrown around his teenage sons. He had recently become the world’s second centibillionaire, his net worth hovering somewhere around $112 billion, about half of what it is today. That number, previously unimaginable, had made him unique on a planet of 8 billion people, and you could feel it in the room. Even the richest and most famous among us were drawn to the energy of this impossible wealth.
paragraphs.zh: 那周末，贝索斯无处不在：穿着紧身T恤，笑得过于夸张，双臂搂着他十几岁的儿子。他最近刚成为世界第二位百亿富豪，净资产徘徊在约1,120亿美元，约为如今的一半。这个此前几乎难以想象的数字，在8亿人口的地球上使他与众不同，你在现场能清晰感受到这种氛围。即便是我们当中最富有、最著名的人，也都被这种不可思议的财富气场吸引住了。

paragraphs.en: Martin Baron: Where Jeff Bezos went wrong with The Washington Post
paragraphs.zh: 马丁·巴伦：杰夫·贝索斯在《华盛顿邮报》上的失误

paragraphs.en: Though we didn’t know it at the time, Bezos’s first marriage would be over a few weeks later. My defining impression of his wife that weekend was sadness, even though Bezos made a big show of performing the role of family man. In hindsight, it is that performance that sticks with me. The Jeff Bezos of 2018 acted as if he still believed that people’s impression of him mattered, that his financial and social value could be affected by negative publicity. He still believed that his actions had consequences. He had not yet freed himself—the way Daniel Plainview freed himself—from the rules of men.
paragraphs.zh: 当时我们还不知道，贝索斯与妻子的婚姻在几个星期后就要结束。那周末给我留下最深刻印象的，是他妻子脸上的悲伤，尽管贝索斯当时极力扮演着家庭男人的角色。事后看来，正是这种“表演”最让人印象深刻。2018年的贝索斯仿佛仍然相信，人们对他的看法仍然重要；相信他的财务和社会价值会受到负面舆论的影响。他还认为自己的行为会带来后果。他当时还没有像丹尼尔·普莱恩维尤那样摆脱对他人的规则束缚。

paragraphs.en: Eight years later, Bezos and two of the world’s other richest men—Mark Zuckerberg and Elon Musk—have clearly left the world of consequences behind. They float in a sensory-deprivation tank the size of the planet, in which their actions are only ever judged by themselves.
paragraphs.zh: 八年后，贝索斯与另外两位世界最富有的人——马克·扎克伯格和埃隆·马斯克——显然已经把“后果”这个概念抛在了身后。他们在一个近乎地球大小的感官剥夺罐里漂浮，那里只有他们自己来评判自己的行为。

paragraphs.en: The closer I’ve gotten to the world of wealth, the more I understand that being truly rich doesn’t mean amassing enough money to afford superyachts, private jets, or a million acres of land. It means that everything becomes effectively free. Any asset can be acquired but nothing can ever be lost, because for soon-to-be trillionaires, no level of loss could significantly change their global standing or personal power. For them, the word failure has ceased to mean anything.
paragraphs.zh: 我越是接近财富世界，就越能理解，真正的富有并不意味着攒够钱去买超豪华游艇、私人飞机或一百万英亩土地。它意味着一切都几乎变得是免费的。任何资产都可以被获得，但几乎不会有任何东西真正丧失，因为对于即将成为万亿富翁的人而言，再大的损失也不会实质性改变他们在全球的地位或个人权力。对他们来说，“失败”这个词已经失去了意义。

paragraphs.en: Adam Serwer: How America chose not to hold the powerful to account
paragraphs.zh: 亚当·塞尔弗：美国为何未能让权贵承担责任

paragraphs.en: This sense of invulnerability has deep psychological ramifications. If everything is free and nothing matters, then the world and other people exist only to be acted upon, if they are acknowledged at all. This is different from classic narcissism, in which a grandiose but fragile self-image can mask deep insecurity. What I’m talking about is a self-definition in which the individual grows to the size of the universe, and the universe vanishes. Asked recently if there is any check on his power, President Trump—himself a billionaire, and by far the richest president in American history—said, “Yeah, there is one thing. My own morality. My own mind. It’s the only thing that can stop me.” Not domestic or international law, not the will of the voters, not God or the centuries-old morality of civic and religious life.
paragraphs.zh: 这种无敌感有着深远的心理后果。如果一切都是免费的、没有什么真正重要，那么这个世界和其他人就只是为了被你“使用”而存在——如果他们的存在还被你承认的话。这不同于经典意义上的自恋；后者是宏大的、自我膨胀却又脆弱的自我形象，往往掩盖了深层的不安全感。我说的是一种自我定义：个体膨胀到宇宙的尺度，而宇宙却消失了。特朗普最近被问及是否有任何力量可以制约他的权力时——他自己就是亿万富翁，且是美国历史上迄今最富有的总统——他说道："是的，有一样。我的道德感。我的思想。只有它能阻止我。" 不是国内法，不是国际法，不是选民的意志，不是上帝，也不是几百年来公民与宗教生活中的道德准则。

paragraphs.en: Decades of research in developmental psychology have shown that moral reasoning develops through consequences—not punishment, necessarily, but experiencing the effects of your actions on others, receiving honest feedback, having to accommodate reality as it actually is rather than as you wish it to be. It’s not that the wealthy become evil; it’s that their environment stops teaching them the things that nonwealthy people are forced to learn simply by living in a world that pushes back. When you can buy your way out of any mistake, when you can fire anyone who disagrees with you, when your social circle consists entirely of people who need something from you, the basic mechanism by which humans learn that other people are real goes dark.
paragraphs.zh: 发展心理学几十年的研究表明，道德推理是通过后果逐步形成的——不一定是惩罚，而是亲身体验自己行为对他人的影响、获得诚实反馈、并且不得不按现实本来的样子而非你希望的样子来适应现实。问题不在于富人会变坏，而在于他们的环境不再教会他们那些非富人因为生活在会“反噬”他们的世界里而不得不学会的东西。当你可以用金钱弥补任何错误，当你可以开除任何与你意见不合的人，当你的社交圈全是那些需要你的人时，人类用来学习“其他人是真实存在的”这件事的基本机制，就会逐渐失灵。

paragraphs.en: Thomas Chatterton Williams: The very powerful men who think introspection is dumb
paragraphs.zh: 托马斯·查特顿·威廉姆斯：那些认为自省很蠢的超级有权势的人

paragraphs.en: When Peter Thiel said, “I no longer believe that freedom and democracy are compatible,” he wasn’t talking about your freedom. He was talking about his own. You don’t exist. When Musk took a chainsaw to the federal government as part of the inside joke he called DOGE, he did so with the air of a man who believed that nothing matters—poverty, chaos, human suffering. He was having fun. It didn’t even matter that the entire destructive exercise ultimately yielded no practical financial gains. For him, the outcome was a foregone conclusion: He could only win, because losing had lost its meaning.
paragraphs.zh: 当彼得·蒂尔说“我不再相信自由与民主是兼容的”时，他说的并不是你的自由，而是他自己的自由。你并不存在。马斯克在把联邦政府当靶子，用他那场戏谑般的“DOGE”行动大开大合时，正带着一种相信一切都不重要的人的姿态——贫困、混乱、人类痛苦都不重要。他在享受这个过程。最终这场破坏性行为并没有带来任何实际的经济收益，这一点甚至对他来说也无关紧要。对他来说，结果早已注定：他只能赢，因为输已经失去了意义。

paragraphs.en: Since the 2024 election, there has been a philosophical shift on the right, and especially among tech billionaires, to vilify the idea of empathy. Musk has called empathy “the fundamental weakness of Western civilization.” He sees it as a weapon wielded by liberal society to bludgeon otherwise rational people into operating against their own interests. Empathy is something done to you by others—a vulnerability they exploit, a back door through which they gain access to your resources and will. This rejection of empathy as a human value gives cover to people who don’t want to feel anything at all. If empathy is the problem, then lack of it isn’t a deficiency—it’s an advantage.
paragraphs.zh: 自2024年大选以来，右派，尤其是科技亿万富翁，出现了一种哲学转向，开始妖魔化同理心。马斯克称同理心是“西方文明的根本弱点”。他认为它是自由主义社会挥舞的一把武器，把本来理性的人打击到违背自身利益地行事。同理心是别人对你施加的一种作用——他们会利用你身上的脆弱性，这是进入你的资源和意志的一扇后门。这种对同理心作为人类价值的否定，为那些根本不想感受任何东西的人提供了借口。如果同理心是问题，那么缺乏同理心就不是缺陷，而是一种优势。

paragraphs.en: Elizabeth Bruenig: The conservative attack on empathy
paragraphs.zh: 伊丽莎白·布鲁尼格：保守派对同理心的抨击

paragraphs.en: I finally met Bezos on the last day of Campfire, at lunch, after my wife had broken her wrist. I went over to thank him for having us, and he asked how our Campfire experience had been. I told him that it was great, but that unfortunately my wife had broken her wrist that morning when she slipped on the wet grass while kicking a ball with our 6-year-old son.
paragraphs.zh: 我终于在Campfire的最后一天与贝索斯见面，那是在午餐时，妻子手腕骨折之后。我去向他道谢，感谢他邀请我们参加活动，他问我在Campfire的体验如何。我告诉他一切都很棒，可遗憾的是妻子那天早上在海滩草地上踢球时，和我们的6岁儿子一起滑倒，结果把手腕扭伤了。

paragraphs.en: The night before, we’d all stood by the pool at the beach club watching a cadre of synchronized swimmers execute a flawless water routine. I had spoken with a famous novelist, who said, “I just don’t understand why I’m here.” A famous rock star was about to start an acoustic set. The famous chef had made paella. Somewhere deep under my skin, a brutal pox was beginning to form.
paragraphs.zh: 前一天晚上，我们都站在沙滩俱乐部的泳池边，看着一队花样游泳运动员完成了一套几乎完美的水上编排。我和一位著名小说家聊过天，他说：“我就是不明白我为什么会在这里。”一位著名摇滚歌手正准备开始一场原声表演。那位著名厨师做了海鲜饭。就在我内心深处，不知何时，一种锋利而残忍的厌恶感开始萌生。

paragraphs.en: The next morning, my wife fell, and I found myself in a black SUV with a team of private-security contractors, who whisked us to the back entrance of a Santa Barbara emergency room, where she was seen and treated right away. We made it back in time to watch the Supreme Court justice Zoom in from Washington, D.C.
paragraphs.zh: 第二天早上，我妻子摔了一跤，我和一队私人安保承包团队一起被带上了一辆黑色SUV，他们把我们送到圣芭芭拉一家急诊室的后门，她得到了立即检查和处理。我们赶在时间内回到家里，看了最高法院一位大法官从华盛顿特区参加的 Zoom 视频会议。

paragraphs.en: How was your Campfire? Bezos asked me an hour later, and because I am an honest person, and because I have been a host myself, I decided he would want to know that there had been a problem, but that his team had reacted quickly and been extremely helpful. To be clear, I was in no way blaming him, nor was I shaking down the richest man on Earth. Instead, I was simply offering Bezos, also a husband and father, a brief human connection.
paragraphs.zh: 一个小时后，贝索斯问我“你们的 Campfire 进展如何？”。我本性坦率，而且自己也做过东道主，所以我决定告诉他确实出过点小事，但他的团队反应很快，帮助非常到位。要说清楚，我并没有在指责他，也不是在敲该地球上最富有的人。我的意思只是想给贝索斯，一个丈夫和父亲，提供一次简短而真实的人情互动。

paragraphs.en: But when I told him what had happened, Bezos looked horrified. He did not say “I’m so sorry.” He did not say “Do you need anything?” Instead, he made a face, and in an instant, an aide came and whisked him away. When presented with the opportunity for empathy, even performative empathy, he chose escape.
paragraphs.zh: 但当我把发生的事告诉他时，贝索斯看起来惊慌失措。他没有说“我很抱歉”。也没说“你需要什么吗？”他只是做了个表情，下一秒，一个助理就出现了并把他带走了。即便是一个表达同情的机会，就算是“表演式同情”，他也选择了逃避。

paragraphs.en: A few hours later, on the private plane home, a famous movie producer offered my wife a blanket. My children’s faces were covered in spots. Under my fingernails, red welts were beginning to rise.
paragraphs.zh: 几小时后，在飞回家的私人飞机上，一位著名电影制作人给我妻子盖了一条毯子。我的孩子们的脸上起满了红点。我指甲里也开始冒出红色的丘疹。

paragraphs.en: The world has always been run by rich men. The robber barons of the Gilded Age were known for their ruthlessness in the accumulation of wealth—hiring Pinkertons to shoot striking unionists. But they directly engaged with the world around them, using their wealth and power to muscle it into its most profitable form. And although today’s billionaires are clearly manipulating society to maximize their own profit, something else is also happening—a disassociation from the reality of cause and effect, from meaning and history. These men no longer feel the need to change the world in order to succeed, because their success is guaranteed, no matter what happens to the rest of us.
paragraphs.zh: 这个世界一直都是富人主导的。镀金时代的“强盗资本家”以其攫取财富时的冷酷著称——雇佣平克顿特工去射杀罢工的工会成员。但他们与周围的世界仍是直接接触的，并且动用财富与权力把现实世界推向最有利可图的形态。而今天的亿万富翁虽然显然在操纵社会以放大自身利润，但还有另一种变化正在发生：与因果关系、意义和历史现实的脱节。正因为他们的成功几乎注定不会受影响，这些人不再觉得必须通过改变世界来获得成功，无论其他人的命运如何。

paragraphs.en: “I’m finished,” yells Daniel Plainview, perched happily on the polished floor of his own celestial kingdom. Though he has just committed a crime, he has never felt so free.
paragraphs.zh: “我完了，”丹尼尔·普莱恩维尤喊道，他正开心地坐在自己这片“天国”般宫殿里闪亮的地板上。尽管他刚刚犯下罪行，他却从未感到如此自由。

paragraphs.en: This article appears in the May 2026 print edition with the headline “Everything Is Free and Nothing Matters.”
paragraphs.zh: 这篇文章出现在2026年5月的纸质版中，标题是“任何事都免费，什么都不重要”。
